channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster agree to settle antitrust lawsuit with Justice Department
item.description: The deal, which must still be approved by a judge, is scheduled to be made public Monday in federal court in Manhattan, said a senior Justice Department official who spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss the terms of the still-undisclosed agreement.

item.title: Global markets slide over oil fears as governments scramble to contain barrel prices
item.description: The Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq composite index and S&P 500 each were down about 1 percent to start the day. Earlier, Japan’s Nikkei index plunged about 5 percent and European stock markets shed 1 to 2 percent. Meanwhile, analysts warned of U.S. pump prices of $4 a gallon, an important psychological barrier that, unlike most economic numbers, is broadcast from street corners nationwide.

item.title: Israeli officials are growing concerned, David Ignatius writes
item.description: A bombing campaign nearing its military goals in Iran leaves the hardest questions unanswered.

item.title: A job shakeup in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Monday points.

item.title: After slashing federal jobs, Trump administration ramps up hiring
item.description: The reversal reflects a retreat from one of the president's early priorities. It could give the White House greater influence over a civilian workforce of 2 million.

item.title: Oil prices soar above $100 over fears of prolonged Middle East conflict
item.description: President Donald Trump called surging oil costs — which are now at their highest level since 2022 — a “very small price to pay.” Group of 7 leaders are set to convene on Monday to assess the economic fallout.

item.title: Iran. War-proof your budget. Federal hiring. Detention centers. Tech thieves. Difficult people. Northern lights. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: ‘Hasslers’ in your life may affect your longevity, study suggests
item.description: New research suggests that difficult people, a.k.a. “hasslers,” contribute to chronic stress and elevate epigenetic biomarkers associated with aging.

item.title: Iran chooses Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s son as supreme leader, state media says
item.description: The selection of Mojtaba Khamenei, a powerful regime insider deeply intertwined with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, sends a strong message of defiance to the Trump administration amid the U.S.-Israeli military campaign.

item.title: U.S. military announces death of seventh service member in war with Iran
item.description: Central Command said the service member, who was not identified, was seriously wounded in a March 1 attack on U.S. troops in Saudi Arabia and passed away of their injuries on Saturday.

item.title: Bird rarely seen in the United States sends people flocking to Virginia
item.description: The red-flanked bluetail has appeared at a park near the Potomac River, marking only the second time the species has been seen east of the Rockies.

item.title: War with Iran spreading economic damage far beyond oil and gas markets
item.description: The conflict has hit Europe and Asia harder than the United States, with shipping costs and energy prices rising.

item.title: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.
item.description: Play now to finish out your score for the week.

item.title: Why do airlines hate basic economy passengers?
item.description: The nation's top carriers continue to chip away at perks. The latest: no more earning loyalty miles.

item.title: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now
item.description: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now

item.title: Video of ICE shooting of Texas man raises questions about government claim
item.description: An Immigration and Customs Enforcement official said Ruben Martinez “intentionally ran over” an agent before being killed by another agent last March.

item.title: Federal judge rules Kari Lake unlawfully ran U.S. media agency, voiding Voice of America layoffs
item.description: The decision is the latest in a string of legal defeats to the Trump administration’s year-long effort to dismantle the federal agency that oversees Voice of America and other U.S.-funded international broadcasters, including Radio Free Asia.

item.title: Laden Iranian ships depart Chinese port tied to key military chemicals
item.description: Experts said the vessels are probably carrying a key precursor for rocket fuel, making it notable that Beijing let them sail while the U.S. and Iran are at war.

item.title: After a Grand Canyon adventure, his body began to fail
item.description: A 55-year-old man developed painful swelling that doctors later thought was arthritis. Subtle clues revealed something more rare.

item.title: An autistic man called police for a wellness check. They fatally shot him.
item.description: Alex LaMorie, 25, had recently moved to a housing community in Maryland for people with disabilities, and was shot after police say he approached officers while holding a knife.

item.title: Regular gas could hit $4 a gallon. Here’s how to war-proof your budget, our columnist writes.
item.description: As the Iran conflict leads to rising gas prices, these tips can help you save money and maintain some budget predictability.

item.title: A French grandmother took an American road trip — and was struck by what she saw
item.description: Monique Uffholtz and her grandson, photographer Arnaud Montagard, drove more than 2,000 miles on back roads through Nevada, Utah, Arizona, California, Colorado and New Mexico.

item.title: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.
item.description: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.

item.title: Long-delayed Jan. 6 plaque honoring police quietly erected overnight at Capitol
item.description: The memorial was required by law to be installed by March 2023. This morning, while most of Washington slept, it appeared.

item.title: Classified intelligence report warns large-scale war ‘unlikely’ to oust Iran’s regime
item.description: A U.S. report doubts that Iran’s opposition would take power following either a short or extended U.S. military campaign.

item.title: U.S. was the only country in a worldwide survey to say most fellow citizens are bad people
item.description: It’s not clear why so many Americans think their neighbors are immoral, but experts think political polarization has something to do with it.

item.title: The White House is transforming the Iran strikes into a meme war
item.description: By mixing video of real missile strikes with clips from action films and video games, the Trump administration is making light of violent combat.

item.title: Vinay Prasad, controversial top vaccine regulator, to depart FDA for second time
item.description: Prasad, the top regulator overseeing vaccines and complex treatments for difficult diseases, had previously lost his job in July before getting it back less than two weeks later.

item.title: Florida Bar reverses itself, says it is not investigating former interim U.S. attorney Lindsey Halligan
item.description: A spokesperson said the bar’s counsel erroneously stated there was a “pending investigation”; instead, a complaint against Halligan remains at a preliminary stage.

item.title: National Symphony Orchestra director quits, splitting with Kennedy Center
item.description: Jean Davidson cited “a hard year” in her decision to leave the center for a new position in Los Angeles.

item.title: Justice Department targets Cuban officials, aims for indictments
item.description: The effort to bring charges against Cuban officials coincides with President Donald Trump saying that his administration is eyeing Cuba as the next country whose government might be overthrown, following the capture of Venezuela’s Nicolás Maduro in early January and the killing of Iran’s supreme leader on Saturday.

item.title: Carolyn Hax chat: How do I get over years of betrayal from my ex-husband?
item.description: Carolyn Hax chat: How do I get over years of betrayal from my ex-husband?

item.title: Sudden cancellation of Army exercise fuels speculation about combat troop deployments in Middle East
item.description: The unexpected change of plans for the 82nd Airborne Division heightened expectations that U.S. troops could be headed to bolster the Iran war effort. No deployments have been ordered as of Friday, officials said.

item.title: Housing is so expensive that people earning $200,000 qualify for help
item.description: Data shared with The Post shows that home-buying assistance programs across the U.S. have been raising their income threshold requirements.

item.title: 10 questions, and don't forget the bonus wager! Finish off your week with On the Record.
item.description: Think you’ve got what it takes to earn a perfect score? Take today’s longer news quiz and complete your point total for the week.

item.title: The U.S. labor market lost 92,000 jobs in February, a striking loss signaling a warning flag for the economy
item.description: With the unexpected setback, the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4 percent, according to new data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

item.title: Russia is providing Iran with intelligence to target U.S. forces, officials say
item.description: This is the first indication that another major U.S. adversary is participating — even indirectly — in the war, according to three officials familiar with the intelligence.

item.title: Kristi Noem. Russia and Iran. Texas election drama. Trans drivers. Down payments. Tylenol. Baby elephant. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: GOP congressman Tony Gonzales drops reelection bid after admitting affair with aide
item.description: “After deep reflection and with the support of my loving family, I have decided not to seek re-election while serving out the rest of this Congress with the same commitment I’ve always had to my district,” Gonzales said in a late-night statement. Earlier Thursday, House Republican leaders had taken the unusual step of calling on the embattled Texas Republican to step aside over the scandal.

item.title: How the fatal shooting of a third U.S. citizen at the hands of DHS went unnoticed for months
item.description: No state or federal agency disclosed that a Homeland Security Investigations agent had killed Ruben Ray Martinez until it was revealed in a public records request.

item.title: Lindsey Halligan faces investigation by Florida Bar for role in pursuing criminal charges against Trump foes, documents show
item.description: The state bar acknowledged the inquiry in a letter last month to the nonprofit Campaign for Accountability, which had called for disciplinary proceedings against Halligan over her conduct in the prosecutions of former FBI director James B. Comey and New York Attorney General Letitia James. The Justice Department has proposed new regulations that would allow it to intervene in state bar disciplinary actions, which it said have become “weaponized.”

item.title: Kristi Noem’s ouster is an opportunity to reset immigration policy
item.description: Markwayne Mullin’s opportunity to reset immigration policy

item.title: D.C. assistant police chief is placed on leave after improper text messages are found, officials say
item.description: A police spokesman confirmed that Executive Assistant Chief Andre Wright, along with his wife, who also works in the department, were placed on administrative leave.

item.title: Corrects link: House rejects measure to halt further strikes on Iran
item.description: Representatives voted 219-212 to reject the bill. It was the second such measure to fail this week; the Senate blocked a similar bill Wednesday almost entirely along party lines. A previous alert contained the wrong link.

item.title: House rejects measure to halt further strikes on Iran
item.description: Representatives voted 219-212 to reject the bill. It was the second such measure to fail this week; the Senate blocked a similar bill Wednesday almost entirely along party lines.

item.title: House GOP leaders call on Rep. Gonzales to end reelection campaign
item.description: Tony Gonzales, a Republican from Texas, has admitted to an affair with an aide who later died after setting herself on fire.

item.title: DHS Secretary Kristi Noem is out, Trump says, after controversial tenure
item.description: President Donald Trump announced the decision on social media and said Noem would be replaced with Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R-Okla.).

item.title: D.C.’s cherry blossoms will peak between March 29 and April 1, Park Service says
item.description: The National Park Service declares peak bloom when 70 percent of the blossoms around the Tidal Basin, the reservoir on the National Mall, have opened.

item.title: White House ballroom vote, a final procedural hurdle, has been delayed due to significant public input, federal commission says
item.description: The National Capital Planning Commission’s chair cited the amount of public comment as the reason for the delay.

item.title: Now streaming! The Clinton depositions were the week’s best new drama, our columnist writes.
item.description: Your favorite characters are back, and you won’t believe what the writers have in store for them this season.
